items.title: Standardizing features before logistic regression
items.scenario: You have a training matrix `X` with shape `(50_000, 12)` — 50,000 customer rows and 12 numeric features. Before fitting logistic regression, you subtract each column mean and divide by each column standard deviation so features share comparable scales.
items.explanation: With vectorization, `X = (X - X.mean(axis=0)) / X.std(axis=0)` runs in compiled code across all 600,000 values. Broadcasting applies the length-12 mean and std vectors across every row without an explicit loop. A Python loop over rows and columns would spend most of its time in interpreter overhead.

items.title: Clipping sensor readings in an IoT pipeline
items.scenario: A batch of temperature readings arrives as a 1-D NumPy array. Values below -40 °C or above 120 °C are sensor errors and must be clipped to the valid range before downstream anomaly detection.
items.explanation: `readings = np.clip(readings, -40.0, 120.0)` applies the bounds to the entire array in one vectorized call. This is clearer and faster than iterating with `if` statements, and it preserves the array dtype for later matrix operations.

items.title: Building a pairwise distance grid with broadcasting
items.scenario: You have 200 embedding vectors of dimension 64 and need a quick sanity-check heatmap of squared Euclidean distances between every pair — a common step when validating a new text encoder before indexing.
items.explanation: Reshape to `A = embeddings[:, np.newaxis, :]` and `B = embeddings[np.newaxis, :, :]`, then compute `((A - B) ** 2).sum(axis=-1)`. Broadcasting forms a `(200, 200, 64)` temporary implicitly aligned so each pair of rows is compared without nested Python loops.

items.title: Masking missing values during train/validation split
items.scenario: A numeric feature column contains `np.nan` for 3% of rows. You want to replace NaNs with the column median on the training fold only, then apply the same median to the validation fold to avoid leakage.
items.explanation: Compute `median = np.nanmedian(train_col)`, then `train_col = np.where(np.isnan(train_col), median, train_col)` and the same on validation. Boolean masks (`np.isnan`) and `np.where` vectorize the conditional update — no row-by-row `if` in Python.
